title: Josephï؟½s Dreams And Reubenï؟½s Brethren (A Recital In Six Chapters)
content:
CHAPTER I

آ آ آ آ I cannot blame old Israel yet,
آ آ آ آ For I am not a sage,
آ آ آ آ I shall not know until I get
آ آ آ آ The son of my old age.
آ آ آ آ The mysteries of this Vale of Tears
آ آ آ آ We will perchance explain
آ آ آ آ When we have lived a thousand years
آ آ آ آ And died and come again.

آ آ آ آ No doubt old Jacob acted mean
آ آ آ آ Towards his fatherï؟½s son;
آ آ آ آ But other hands were none too clean,
آ آ آ آ When all is said and done.
آ آ آ آ There were some things that had to be
آ آ آ آ In those old days, ï؟½tis true,
آ آ آ آ But with old Jacobï؟½s history
آ آ آ آ This tale has nought to do.

آ آ آ آ (They had to keep the birth-rate up,
آ آ آ آ And populate the land,
آ آ آ آ They did it, too, by simple means
آ آ آ آ That we canï؟½t understand.
آ آ آ آ The Patriarchsï؟½ way of fixing things
آ آ آ آ Would make an awful row,
آ آ آ آ And Sarahï؟½s plain, straightforward plan
آ آ آ آ Would never answer now.)

آ آ آ آ This is a tale of simple men
آ آ آ آ And one precocious boy,
آ آ آ آ A spoilt kid, and, as usual,
آ آ آ آ His fatherï؟½s hope and joy
آ آ آ آ (It mostly is the way in which
آ آ آ آ The younger sons behave
آ آ آ آ That brings the old manï؟½s grey hairs down
آ آ آ آ In sorrow to the grave.)

آ آ آ آ Old Jacob loved the whelp, and made,
آ آ آ آ While meaning to be kind,
آ آ آ آ A coat of many colours that
آ آ آ آ Would strike a nigger blind!
آ آ آ آ It struck the brethren green, ï؟½twas said,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d take a pinch of salt
آ آ آ آ Their coats had coloured patches too,
آ آ آ آ But that was not their fault.

آ آ آ آ Young Joseph had a soft thing on,
آ آ آ آ And, humbugged from his birth,
آ آ آ آ You may depend he worked the thing
آ آ آ آ For all that it was worth.
آ آ آ آ And that he grafted not but crowed,
آ آ آ آ You donï؟½t need to be told,
آ آ آ آ And he was mighty cocky, with
آ آ آ آ His ï؟½Lo!ï؟½ and his ï؟½Behold!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He took in all his brothers said,
آ آ آ آ And went and told his Dad,
آ آ آ آ And then, when someone split on him,
آ آ آ آ No wonder they were mad.
آ آ آ آ But still he wasnï؟½t satisfied,
آ آ آ آ And it would almost seem
آ آ آ آ He itched to rile his brethren, for
آ آ آ آ He went and dreamed a dream,

آ آ آ آ And told it to his brothers straight
آ آ آ آ (So Genesis believes):,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Lo! we were working in the field,
آ آ آ آ And we were binding sheaves,
آ آ آ آ And my sheaf rose and stood upright,
آ آ آ آ And, straightway, for a sign,
آ آ آ آ Your sheaves came round about and made
آ آ آ آ Obeisance to mine!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The brethren stared and made comment
آ آ آ آ In words that were not mild,
آ آ آ آ And when the meaning dawned on them
آ آ آ آ You bet that they were wild!
آ آ آ آ And Joseph left those angry men
آ آ آ آ To boil and blow off steam,
آ آ آ آ And ambled, chuckling, home agen
آ آ آ آ To dream another dream.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Behold! Iï؟½ve dreamed a dream once more!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He told ï؟½em, frank and free,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½The sun, moon, and eleven stars
آ آ آ آ Have likewise bowed to me!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ (Perhaps Astronomy has changed
آ آ آ آ Since Joseph saw the light,
آ آ آ آ But I have wondered what the sun
آ آ آ آ Was doing out at night.)

آ آ آ آ And when they dropped!, you never heard,
آ آ آ آ In sheds or shanty bars,
آ آ آ آ Such awful language as escaped
آ آ آ آ From those eleven stars.
آ آ آ آ You know how Jacob-Israel loved
آ آ آ آ His hopeful youngest pup;
آ آ آ آ But, when he heard the latest dream,
آ آ آ آ It shook the old man up.

آ آ آ آ But Joseph talked his father round,
آ آ آ آ Who humoured every whim
آ آ آ آ (Perhaps old Jacob half-believed
آ آ آ آ They would bow down to him):
آ آ آ آ But, anyway, as always was,
آ آ آ آ He backed the youngest son,
آ آ آ آ And sent the others with the sheep
آ آ آ آ Out to the Check-ï؟½em run.



CHAPTER II

آ آ آ آ Now Jacob, with that wondrous tact
آ آ آ آ That doting parents show,
آ آ آ آ Or, anxious for his sons out back,
آ آ آ آ Sent, of all others, Joe!
آ آ آ آ To see if it was well with them
آ آ آ آ (And they were not asleep),
آ آ آ آ With one eye on his brothersï؟½ camp,
آ آ آ آ And one eye on the sheep.

آ آ آ آ He drew a blank on Check-ï؟½em run,
آ آ آ آ Got bushed, too, youï؟½ll be bound.
آ آ آ آ A certain cove, thereï؟½s always one,
آ آ آ آ Saw Joseph mooning round.
آ آ آ آ He asked him how it came to pass,
آ آ آ آ And what it was about,
آ آ آ آ And said, ï؟½Theyï؟½re trav-linï؟½ now for grass
آ آ آ آ In Doothen, further out.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ He also muttered, ï؟½Strike me blue!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ While staring at the clothes,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d never seen a jackaroo
آ آ آ آ With such a coat as Joeï؟½s.
آ آ آ آ He set the nameless on the track,
آ آ آ آ And scratched his head to think,
آ آ آ آ But gave it best, and, riding back,
آ آ آ آ Said firmly, ï؟½Strike me pink!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas blazing hot in Doothen then,
آ آ آ آ The sweat ran down in streams,
آ آ آ آ It melted out the memory
آ آ آ آ Of even Josephï؟½s dreams!
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d had some trouble with the sheep,
آ آ آ آ Some Arabs and a ï؟½shirkï؟½,
آ آ آ آ It was a favourable time
آ آ آ آ For Joe to get to work.

آ آ آ آ They saw him coming, ï؟½afar offï؟½,
آ آ آ آ In this case, you might note,
آ آ آ آ Their eyesight wasnï؟½t wonderful,
آ آ آ آ Considering the coat.
آ آ آ آ And what with sheep, and dust, and flies,
آ آ آ آ And damned shirks in the swim
آ آ آ آ With sheep stealers, the brethren were
آ آ آ آ For absenteeing him.

آ آ آ آ And, add to that, he scared the kine
آ آ آ آ With his infernal coat,
آ آ آ آ They trampled on the sheep and swine
آ آ آ آ And startled every goat.
آ آ آ آ The brethren had to round up then
آ آ آ آ As fast as ass could go,
آ آ آ آ And when they got to camp agen
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d fixed it up for Joe.

آ آ آ آ Save poor old Rube, he had the blight,
آ آ آ آ But, grafting all the same,
آ آ آ آ He only looked on family rows
آ آ آ آ As just a blooming shame.
آ آ آ آ Like many an easy-going man,
آ آ آ آ He had a cunning soul.
آ آ آ آ He said, ï؟½We will not kill the kid,
آ آ آ آ But shove him in a hole,

آ آ آ آ And leave him there to dream oï؟½ thingsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s not the slightest doubt
آ آ آ آ He meant to slip round after dark
آ آ آ آ And pull the youngster out,
آ آ آ آ And fill his gourd and tucker-bag,
آ آ آ آ And tell him ï؟½Not to mindï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And start him on the back-track with
آ آ آ آ A gentle kick behind.

آ آ آ آ Some ï؟½Tothersider prospectors
آ آ آ آ Had been there poking round;
آ آ آ آ You may depend that Reuben knew
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas ï؟½dry and shallow groundï؟½.
آ آ آ آ They dropped young Joseph in a hole,
آ آ آ آ The giddy little goat,
آ آ آ آ And left him there, to cool his heels,
آ آ آ آ Without his overcoat.

آ آ آ آ (Donï؟½t think that Moses, such a whale
آ آ آ آ On dry facts, thought it wet
آ آ آ آ To say, when theyï؟½d chucked Joseph in,
آ آ آ آ It was an empty pit!
آ آ آ آ So many things are preached and said
آ آ آ آ Whereï؟½er the Bible is
آ آ آ آ To prove that Moses never read
آ آ آ آ The ï؟½proofsï؟½ of Genesis.)

آ آ آ آ But letï؟½s get on. While having grub,
آ آ آ آ A brethren sniffed and ï؟½seenï؟½
آ آ آ آ Some Ishmaelites pass through the scrub,
آ آ آ آ Or O-asses, I mean.
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d been right out to Gilead,
آ آ آ آ A rather longish trip,
آ آ آ آ For camel-loads of balm, and myrrh,
آ آ آ آ And spicery for ï؟½Gyp.

آ آ آ آ (Iï؟½ve often seen the Afghans pass
آ آ آ آ With camel strings out back,
آ آ آ آ And thought ï؟½twas somewhat similar
آ آ آ آ On that old Bible track.
آ آ آ آ I donï؟½t know much of balm and myrrh,
آ آ آ آ Whatever they may be,
آ آ آ آ But eï؟½en when sheepskins were not there,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve smelt the spicery.)

آ آ آ آ It was the same in Canaan then
آ آ آ آ As it is here to-day:
آ آ آ آ A sudden thought jerked Judah up
آ آ آ آ For ï؟½brofit ï؟½ straight away.
آ آ آ آ The brethren got on one end too
آ آ آ آ When Judah jumped and said,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Weï؟½ll sell the kid for what he brings!
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s no good when heï؟½s dead.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And, to be short, they being Jews,
آ آ آ آ The ï؟½chosingï؟½ of the earth,
آ آ آ آ They sold him to the Ishmaelites
آ آ آ آ For more than twice his worth.
آ آ آ آ (Some Midianitish auctioneers
آ آ آ آ Were also on the job.)
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas ï؟½twenty bits of silverï؟½, which
آ آ آ آ I sï؟½pose was twenty bob.

آ آ آ آ So they most comfortably got
آ آ آ آ Young Joseph off their hands,
آ آ آ آ For Ishmael never bothered much
آ آ آ آ About receipts or brands.
آ آ آ آ (They spake not of his dreams and cheek,
آ آ آ آ His laziness, or ï؟½skiteï؟½;
آ آ آ آ No doubt they thought the Ishmaelites
آ آ آ آ Would see to that all right.)

آ آ آ آ Then Reuben came; heï؟½d been around
آ آ آ آ To watch the sheep a bit,
آ آ آ آ And on his way back to the camp
آ آ آ آ He slipped round by the pit
آ آ آ آ To give young Joe a drink. He stared,
آ آ آ آ And, thinking Joe was dead,
آ آ آ آ He rent his gown like mad, and ran
آ آ آ آ For ashes for his head.

آ آ آ آ (As if that would do any good!
آ آ آ آ I only know that I
آ آ آ آ Cannot afford to rend my clothes
آ آ آ آ When my relations die.
آ آ آ آ I donï؟½t suppose they would come back,
آ آ آ آ Or that the world would care,
آ آ آ آ If I went howling for a year
آ آ آ آ With ashes in my hair.)

آ آ آ آ You say he counted on a new
آ آ آ آ Rig-out? Yes? And you know
آ آ آ آ That Jacob tore his garment too,
آ آ آ آ So that old cock wonï؟½t crow.
آ آ آ آ Look here! You keep your smart remarks
آ آ آ آ Till after I am gone.
آ آ آ آ I wonï؟½t have Reuben silver-tailed,
آ آ آ آ Nor Pharaoh, later on.

آ آ آ آ The brethren humbugged Reuben well,
آ آ آ آ For fear heï؟½d take the track,
آ آ آ آ And sneak in on the Ishmaelites,
آ آ آ آ And steal young Joseph back,
آ آ آ آ Or fight it out if he was caught,
آ آ آ آ And die, as it might be,
آ آ آ آ Or, at the best, go down with Joe
آ آ آ آ And into slavery.

آ آ آ آ Young Simeon slipped into the scrub,
آ آ آ آ To where the coat was hid,
آ آ آ آ And Judah stayed and wept with Rube,
آ آ آ آ While Levi killed a kid.
آ آ آ آ So they fixed up the wild-beast yarn,
آ آ آ آ And Hebrews sadly note,
آ آ آ آ Considering the price of cloth,
آ آ آ آ They had to spoil the coat.

آ آ آ آ (There was a yam about old Rube
آ آ آ آ That all true men despise,
آ آ آ آ Spread by his fatherï؟½s concubines,
آ آ آ آ A vicious strumpetï؟½s lies.
آ آ آ آ But I believe old Moses was,
آ آ آ آ As we are, well aware
آ آ آ آ That Reuben stood in this last scene
آ آ آ آ The central figure there.)

آ آ آ آ I feel for poor old Israelï؟½s grief,
آ آ آ آ Believing all the same
آ آ آ آ (And not with atheist unbelief)
آ آ آ آ That Jacob was to blame.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas ever so, and shall be done,
آ آ آ آ While one fond fool has breath,
آ آ آ آ Fond folly drives the youngest son
آ آ آ آ To ruin and to death.



آ آ آ آ The caravan went jogging on
آ آ آ آ To Pharaohï؟½s royal town,
آ آ آ آ But Genesis gives no account
آ آ آ آ Of Josephï؟½s journey down.
آ آ آ آ I wouldnï؟½t be surprised to hear
آ آ آ آ He found it pretty rough,
آ آ آ آ But thereï؟½s a bare chance that his hide,
آ آ آ آ As well as cheek, was tough.



آ آ آ آ I see them toiling through the heat,
آ آ آ آ In patches and in dirt,
آ آ آ آ With sand-grooved sandals on their feet,
آ آ آ آ And slaves without a shirt,
آ آ آ آ The dust-caked thirst, the burning ground,
آ آ آ آ The mad and maddening flies,
آ آ آ آ That gathered like black goggles round
آ آ آ آ The piccaninniesï؟½ eyes.



آ آ آ آ The Ishmaelites had tempers brief,
آ آ آ آ And whips of hide and gut,
آ آ آ آ And sometimes, pï؟½raps, for Hagarï؟½s sake,
آ آ آ آ Gave Joe an extra cut.
آ آ آ آ When, fainting by the way, he felt
آ آ آ آ The stimulating touch,
آ آ آ آ I have no doubt he often wished
آ آ آ آ He hadnï؟½t dreamed so much.



آ آ آ آ He didnï؟½t dream much on that trip,
آ آ آ آ Although he thought a lot.
آ آ آ آ However, they got down to ï؟½Gyp
آ آ آ آ In good time, where he got
آ آ آ آ A wash and rest, he needed both,
آ آ آ آ And in the old slave-yard
آ آ آ آ Was sold to Captain Potiphar,
آ آ آ آ Of Pharaohï؟½s body-guard.



INTERLUDE

آ آ آ آ I pause to state that later on
آ آ آ آ (And it seems worth the halt)
آ آ آ آ Smart Judah gat into a mess,
آ آ آ آ Though it was not his fault.
آ آ آ آ And I would only like to say,
آ آ آ آ In this most thankless task,
آ آ آ آ Wives sell to husbands every day,
آ آ آ آ And that without a mask.

آ آ آ آ But, what with family rows and drought,
آ آ آ آ And blessed women too,
آ آ آ آ The fathers of terrestrial tribes
آ آ آ آ Had quite enough to do.
آ آ آ آ They had to graft both day and night,
آ آ آ آ With no rest, save the last,
آ آ آ آ For when they were not grafting they
آ آ آ آ Were populating fast.



CHAPTER III

آ آ آ آ The Captain was a casual man,
آ آ آ آ But seemed a shrewd one too;
آ آ آ آ He got young Josephï؟½s measure soon,
آ آ آ آ And saw what he could do.
آ آ آ آ The Lord was with Joe, Moses said,
آ آ آ آ I know that Joe had pluck,
آ آ آ آ But I believe ï؟½twas mostly check,
آ آ آ آ And his infernal luck.

آ آ آ آ The Captain made him manager,
آ آ آ آ Housekeeper, overseer,
آ آ آ آ And found that this arrangement paid,
آ آ آ آ That much at least is clear.
آ آ آ آ And what with merchants, clerks, and slaves,
آ آ آ آ Joe led a busy life,
آ آ آ آ With one eye on the maid-servants,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Jeamesï؟½ and Pottyï؟½s wife.

آ آ آ آ The Captain seemed a casual man,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½ï؟½Gypï؟½ was on the glide:
آ آ آ آ There was a growing tendency
آ آ آ آ To live and let things slide.
آ آ آ آ He left all things in Josephï؟½s hands,
آ آ آ آ According to old Mose,
آ آ آ آ And knew not what he had besides
آ آ آ آ His tucker and his clothes.

آ آ آ آ I guess he had a shrewd idea,
آ آ آ آ For it is now, as then,
آ آ آ آ The world most often makes mistakes
آ آ آ آ With easy-going men.
آ آ آ آ The Captain often went away
آ آ آ آ For quietness and rest,
آ آ آ آ And, maybe, for some other things,
آ آ آ آ Well, Potiphar knew best.

آ آ آ آ Perhaps the missus knew it too,
آ آ آ آ At least, she should have known,
آ آ آ آ And Joe was handsome, strange, and new,
آ آ آ آ And she was much alone.
آ آ آ آ It seems a funny business now,
آ آ آ آ But I was never there,
آ آ آ آ Perhaps so long as cheques came in
آ آ آ آ The Captain didnï؟½t care.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis strange that Moses, such a whale
آ آ آ آ On details out of joint,
آ آ آ آ Should always come, in such a case,
آ آ آ آ So bluntly to the point.
آ آ آ آ He says Joe had a goodly form,
آ آ آ آ Or person it should be,
آ آ آ آ He says that she cast eyes on Joe,
آ آ آ آ And she said, ï؟½Lie with me.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ It took young Joseph sudden like.
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d heard, while on the run,
آ آ آ آ Of other women who could lie,
آ آ آ آ And in more ways than one;
آ آ آ آ Of men who had been gaoled or hanged,
آ آ آ آ As they are here to-day,
آ آ آ آ (Likewise of lovers who were banged),
آ آ آ آ And so he edged away.

آ آ آ آ She never moved, and so he stayed
آ آ آ آ While she was there to hear,
آ آ آ آ For his infernal vanity
آ آ آ آ Was stronger than his fear.
آ آ آ آ He bragged his opportunity,
آ آ آ آ His strength, and godliness:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½There is no greater in the house
آ آ آ آ Than I.ï؟½ (She made him less.)

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas cant to brag of purity
آ آ آ آ And right in that household,
آ آ آ آ For what was he if not a slave,
آ آ آ آ And basely bought and sold?
آ آ آ آ Unmanly for a man to treat
آ آ آ آ A love-starved woman so,
آ آ آ آ And cowardly to humiliate
آ آ آ آ A spirit thrust so low.

آ آ آ آ She knew that Joseph was a spy
آ آ آ آ On her and all the rest,
آ آ آ آ And this, with his outspoken ï؟½scornï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Made reasons manifest.
آ آ آ آ She had her passions (donï؟½t be shocked,
آ آ آ آ For you have yours, no doubt),
آ آ آ آ And meant to take young Joseph down
آ آ آ آ And pay her husband out.

آ آ آ آ He was a slave, and bought and sold,
آ آ آ آ And I will say right here
آ آ آ آ His preaching was too manifold
آ آ آ آ And glib to be sincere,
آ آ آ آ When youth and ï؟½looksï؟½ turn goody-good,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½ll see it at a glance,
آ آ آ آ They have one eye to womanï؟½s help
آ آ آ آ And both on the main chance.

آ آ آ آ Now, had old Rube been in his place
آ آ آ آ (All honour to his name),
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll swear he would have taken things
آ آ آ آ Exactly as they came,
آ آ آ آ And kept it dark, or fought it out,
آ آ آ آ As the ungodly can,
آ آ آ آ But, whatsoeï؟½er he might have done,
آ آ آ آ He would have been a man!

آ آ آ آ Howbeit, the missus stuck to Joe,
آ آ آ آ Vindictive, vicious, grim,
آ آ آ آ And bore his sermons and rebuffs
آ آ آ آ Until she cornered him. . . .
آ آ آ آ He left his garment in her hand,
آ آ آ آ And gat him out of that. . . .
آ آ آ آ About the merits of the case
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll say no more, thatï؟½s flat.

آ آ آ آ (He knew all right what she was at,
آ آ آ آ And Potiphar was out,
آ آ آ آ He went alone into the house
آ آ آ آ When no one was about.
آ آ آ آ He may have been half-drunk or mad,
آ آ آ آ He certainly was blind,
آ آ آ آ To run no further than the yard,
آ آ آ آ And leave his coat behind!)

آ آ آ آ But, seeing how our laws are fixed,
آ آ آ آ If I get in such dirt,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll straightway get me out of that
آ آ آ آ If, Iï؟½ve to leave my shirt.
آ آ آ آ But I will keep the running up,
آ آ آ آ If I have common-sense,
آ آ آ آ Nor stop this side of Jericho
آ آ آ آ To think of my defence.

آ آ آ آ Joe should have streaked for Suez straight,
آ آ آ آ And tried his luck in flight
آ آ آ آ For Canaan, where they looked on things
آ آ آ آ In quite another light.
آ آ آ آ Old Jacob had experience,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½d have stuck to Joe.
آ آ آ آ He was a match for womenï؟½s lies
آ آ آ آ That flabbergast us so.

آ آ آ آ The missus told the self-same tale,
آ آ آ آ And in the self-same way,
آ آ آ آ As our enfranchised females do
آ آ آ آ In police courts every day.
آ آ آ آ Too cowardly to breathe a breath
آ آ آ آ Against the vilest rip,
آ آ آ آ We send straight men to gaol or death,
آ آ آ آ Just as they did in ï؟½Gyp.

آ آ آ آ Now, Potiphar was wondrous mild,
آ آ آ آ Suspiciously, to say
آ آ آ آ The least. He didnï؟½t operate
آ آ آ آ On Joseph straight away.
آ آ آ آ Perhaps he knew his wife no less
آ آ آ آ Than Joe, yet had regard
آ آ آ آ For his own peace and quietness,
آ آ آ آ So Joe got two yearsï؟½ hard.



CHAPTER IV

آ آ آ آ The Lord was with him, Moses said,
آ آ آ آ Yet his luck didnï؟½t fail,
آ آ آ آ For he got on the right side of
آ آ آ آ The governor of the gaol.
آ آ آ آ Perhaps heï؟½d heard of Mrs P.,
آ آ آ آ And cases like to Joeï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ And knew as much of womanï؟½s work
آ آ آ آ As anybody knows.

آ آ آ آ He made Joe super-lag, a sort
آ آ آ آ Of deputy-retained
آ آ آ آ (The easy-going tendency
آ آ آ آ In Egypt seemed ingrained),
آ آ آ آ Left everything in Josephï؟½s hands,
آ آ آ آ Except, maybe, the keys;
آ آ آ آ And thereafter he let things slide,
آ آ آ آ And smoked his pipe in peace.

آ آ آ آ Now Pharaoh had some trouble with
آ آ آ آ His butler and his cook,
آ آ آ آ But Pharaoh seemed most lenient
آ آ آ آ With asses bought to book,
آ آ آ آ He didnï؟½t cut the weak end off
آ آ آ آ Each absent-minded wretch,
آ آ آ آ But mostly sent the idiots up
آ آ آ آ To ï؟½chokeyï؟½ for a ï؟½stretchï؟½.

آ آ آ آ They found themselves in Josephï؟½s care,
آ آ آ آ And it would almost seem
آ آ آ آ Theyï؟½d got wind of his weaknesses,
آ آ آ آ For each one dreamed a dream.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½They dreamed a dream; both of them. Each
آ آ آ آ Man his dream in one night:
آ آ آ آ Each man according to his dreamï؟½
آ آ آ آ (And his own dream), thatï؟½s right.

آ آ آ آ Next morning they made up their ï؟½mugsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And Joseph, passing through,
آ آ آ آ Asked them if they were feeling cronk,
آ آ آ آ And why they looked so blue?
آ آ آ آ They told him they had dreamed two dreams
آ آ آ آ (One each), and any dunce
آ آ آ آ Can understand how such remarks
آ آ آ آ Would intï؟½rest Joe at once.

آ آ آ آ And there was no interpreter,
آ آ آ آ They said, and that was why
آ آ آ آ Joe said that that belonged to God,
آ آ آ آ But he would have a try.
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve noticed this with ï؟½Christiansï؟½ since,
آ آ آ آ And often thought it odd,
آ آ آ آ They cannot keep their hands from things
آ آ آ آ They say belong to God.

آ آ آ آ The butler dreamed, or, anyway,
آ آ آ آ He said so (understand),
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d made some wine in Pharaohï؟½s cup,
آ آ آ آ And placed it in his hand,
آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh placed the wine inside,
آ آ آ آ I sï؟½pose. But, anyways,
آ آ آ آ There were three branches in the dream,
آ آ آ آ Which were, of course, three days.

آ آ آ آ The butler might have one again,
آ آ آ آ And Joseph, going strong,
آ آ آ آ By evil chance get wind of it,
آ آ آ آ And diagnose it wrong!
آ آ آ آ The cook had been the butlerï؟½s mate,
آ آ آ آ And he thought (was it odd?)
آ آ آ آ That nightmare students such as Joe
آ آ آ آ Were safer far in quod.

آ آ آ آ He did repent him of his fault,
آ آ آ آ Though it was rather late,
آ آ آ آ For Pharaohï؟½s dreams had called a halt,
آ آ آ آ A reason of some weight.
آ آ آ آ The butler hoped to score, but ï؟½twas
آ آ آ آ A risky thing to do,
آ آ آ آ And you will wonder, later on,
آ آ آ آ If Joe ï؟½forgatï؟½ him too.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas plain to any fool, so Joe
آ آ آ آ Said: ï؟½Yet within three days
آ آ آ آ Shall Pharaoh lift thine head up, and
آ آ آ آ Restore thee to thy place.
آ آ آ آ Thou shalt deliver Pharaohï؟½s cup
آ آ آ آ Into his hand once more.
آ آ آ آ (And he shall drink the liquor down
آ آ آ آ Just as it was before.)

آ آ آ آ ï؟½But promise, when thou art all right,
آ آ آ آ And nothing is amiss,
آ آ آ آ To speak to Pharaoh of my case,
آ آ آ آ And get me out of this.
آ آ آ آ For I was kidnapped, likewise gaoled,
آ آ آ آ For nothing that I know.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ (And, granting his celibacy,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twould seem that that was so.)

آ آ آ آ The cook, he was a godless cook,
آ آ آ آ But quietly he stood,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Til Josephï؟½s inspiration came,
آ آ آ آ And he saw it was good.
آ آ آ آ And then his dream he did unfold,
آ آ آ آ All straight and unrehearsed
آ آ آ آ (Without a ï؟½Lo!ï؟½ or a ï؟½Behold!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Or windmill business first):

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½d three old baskets on me ï؟½ed,
آ آ آ آ Now I ainï؟½t tellinï؟½ lies!,
آ آ آ آ The top ï؟½un full of fancy bread
آ آ آ آ Anï؟½ pork ï؟½nï؟½ kidney pies.
آ آ آ آ I didnï؟½t bother looking up,
آ آ آ آ For it was blazinï؟½ ï؟½ot,
آ آ آ آ There come a flock of crimson crows
آ آ آ آ And scoffed the bleedinï؟½ lot.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The cook he was a clever cook,
آ آ آ آ But heï؟½d been on the spree,
آ آ آ آ He put the case as man to man,
آ آ آ آ And put it frank and free.
آ آ آ آ He patted Joseph on the back,
آ آ آ آ Told him to go ahead,
آ آ آ آ And Joseph met the cook half way,
آ آ آ آ And (man to man) he said:

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Within three days shall Pharaoh lift
آ آ آ آ Thine head from off of thee,
آ آ آ آ And he shall hang thee by the heels
آ آ آ آ To the most handy tree.
آ آ آ آ A flock of crows shall pick thy bones
آ آ آ آ (And, to be trebly sure,
آ آ آ آ His slaves shall pound them up with stones
آ آ آ آ And use them for manure).ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The butler passed an anxious night,
آ آ آ آ He wanted matters fixed,
آ آ آ آ For what if Joeï؟½s prescriptions should
آ آ آ آ By some fool chance get mixed?
آ آ آ آ The cook, who was a careless cook,
آ آ آ آ Wrote scoff words on the wall,
آ آ آ آ But, when the time was up, he wished
آ آ آ آ He hadnï؟½t dreamed at all.

آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh gave a feast, heï؟½d got
آ آ آ آ Another chef this trip,
آ آ آ آ And his old butler he restored
آ آ آ آ Unto his butlership;
آ آ آ آ But hanged the cook. And after that,
آ آ آ آ Or this is how it seems,
آ آ آ آ The butler straight away forgat
آ آ آ آ Young Joseph and his dreams.

آ آ آ آ And maybe he was wise, for all
آ آ آ آ That anybody knows,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d seen the headless baker hanged,
آ آ آ آ And picked clean by the crows.
آ آ آ آ It struck him, too, when looking back
آ آ آ آ While calm and free from cares,
آ آ آ آ That Joseph had an off hand way
آ آ آ آ Of fixing up nightmares.



CHAPTER V

آ آ آ آ The gaol did Joseph little good,
آ آ آ آ Except by starts and fits,
آ آ آ آ But saved old Egypt for a while,
آ آ آ آ And brightened up his wits.
آ آ آ آ And, lest you thought me most unjust
آ آ آ آ In matters lately gone,
آ آ آ آ You read and know how holy Joe
آ آ آ آ Sold Egypt later on.

آ آ آ آ Her weather prophets were as good
آ آ آ آ As ours are, every bit,
آ آ آ آ But Pharaoh took to dreaming dreams,
آ آ آ آ And made a mess of it.
آ آ آ آ (And but for that, I do not care
آ آ آ آ What anybody thinks,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d not have lost my overcoat,
آ آ آ آ And watch and chain, and links.)

آ آ آ آ Now Josephï؟½s and the prisonersï؟½ dreams
آ آ آ آ Were plain as dreams could be,
آ آ آ آ And more especially Pharaohï؟½s dreams,
آ آ آ آ As far as I can see,
آ آ آ آ The same man who invented them
آ آ آ آ Could well have read them too,
آ آ آ آ But any third-rate showman knows
آ آ آ آ That that would never do.

آ آ آ آ There must be ï؟½Loï؟½sï؟½, ï؟½Beholdsï؟½, and ï؟½Yetsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½It must come to passï؟½,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Til floods are gone, and tanks are dry,
آ آ آ آ And thereï؟½s no crops nor grass.
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½Likewiseï؟½, ï؟½Alsoesï؟½, ï؟½Says untoï؟½,
آ آ آ آ And countless weary ï؟½Andsï؟½,
آ آ آ آ Until Japan sends Chinamen
آ آ آ آ To irrigate the lands.

آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh must take off his ring
آ آ آ آ (The one from off his hand),
آ آ آ آ To put upon Joeï؟½s little fin,
آ آ آ آ That all might understand.
آ آ آ آ And they must ride in chariots,
آ آ آ آ Have banquets everywhere,
آ آ آ آ And launch trips up the Hawkesbury,
آ آ آ آ To see Australia there.

آ آ آ آ (I dreamed last night that cattle fed
آ آ آ آ Along the river flats,
آ آ آ آ They bore the brands of all the States,
آ آ آ آ And looked like ï؟½Queensland fatsï؟½.
آ آ آ آ And lo! a mob of strangers came,
آ آ آ آ All bones, from horn to heel,
آ آ آ آ But they had nostrils breathing flame,
آ آ آ آ And they had horns of steel.

آ آ آ آ I dreamed that seven sheep were shorn
آ آ آ آ That went by seven tracks,
آ آ آ آ And strove to live the winter through
آ آ آ آ With sackcloth on their backs.
آ آ آ آ And lo! I dreamed, from east and west
آ آ آ آ There came two blades of heat,
آ آ آ آ One blackened all the towns like fire,
آ آ آ آ Like drought one burnt the wheat.

آ آ آ آ A black slave and a white slave laid
آ آ آ آ A golden carpet down,
آ آ آ آ And yellow guards stood round about,
آ آ آ آ And he that came was brown.
آ آ آ آ Men slaved beneath the whip in pits,
آ آ آ آ Who now slave willingly,
آ آ آ آ They sold their birthright for a ï؟½scoreï؟½.
آ آ آ آ Now read those dreams for me!)

آ آ آ آ But Joseph fixed up Pharaohï؟½s dreams
آ آ آ آ As quick as I can tell,
آ آ آ آ And, for Australiaï؟½s sake, I wish
آ آ آ آ That mine were fixed as well,
آ آ آ آ And nationalized from trusts and rings
آ آ آ آ And shady covenants;
آ آ آ آ But, we have thirteen little kings
آ آ آ آ Of thirteen Parliaments.

آ آ آ آ The years of plenty soon run out,
آ آ آ آ And, from the cricket score,
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ll turn to face the years of drought
آ آ آ آ And might-be years of war.
آ آ آ آ With neither money, men, nor guns,
آ آ آ آ With nothing but despair,
آ آ آ آ But I get tired of printing truths
آ آ آ آ For use, no matter where.

آ آ آ آ Joe said to seek a wise man out,
آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh took the Jew,
آ آ آ آ Adventurers fix up our dreams,
آ آ آ آ And we elect them too.
آ آ آ آ I mean no slur on any tribe
آ آ آ آ (My best friend was a Yid),
آ آ آ آ But we let boodlers shape our ends,
آ آ آ آ And just as Pharaoh did.

آ آ آ آ But Joseph did spy out the land,
آ آ آ آ If not for his own good
آ آ آ آ (He only boodled on the grand,
آ آ آ آ It must be understood).
آ آ آ آ He made a corner first in wheat,
آ آ آ آ And did it thoroughly,
آ آ آ آ No ï؟½trustï؟½ has ever seen since then
آ آ آ آ So great a shark as he.

آ آ آ آ And when the fearful famine came,
آ آ آ آ And corn was in demand,
آ آ آ آ He grabbed, in Godï؟½s and Pharaohï؟½s name,
آ آ آ آ The money, stock, and land.
آ آ آ آ (He knew the drought was very bad
آ آ آ آ In Canaan; crops were gone;
آ آ آ آ But never once inquired how his
آ آ آ آ Old Dad was getting on.)



CHAPTER VI

آ آ آ آ And after many barren years
آ آ آ آ Of spirit-breaking work,
آ آ آ آ I see the brethren journeying down
آ آ آ آ From Canaanï؟½s West-oï؟½-Bourke
آ آ آ آ And into Egypt to buy corn,
آ آ آ آ As, at this very hour,
آ آ آ آ My brethren toil through blazing heat
آ آ آ آ The weary miles for flour.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas noble of our Joseph then,
آ آ آ آ The Governor of the land,
آ آ آ آ To bait those weary, simple men,
آ آ آ آ With ï؟½moniesï؟½ in their hand;
آ آ آ آ To gratify his secret spite,
آ آ آ آ As only cowards can;
آ آ آ آ And preen his blasted vanity,
آ آ آ آ And strike through Benjamin.

آ آ آ آ He put a cup in Bennyï؟½s sack,
آ آ آ آ And sent them on their way,
آ آ آ آ And sent the Pleece to bring ï؟½em back
آ آ آ آ Before theyï؟½d gone a day.
آ آ آ آ The constable was well aware
آ آ آ آ Of Josephï؟½s little plan,
آ آ آ آ And most indignant when he caught
آ آ آ آ The wretched caravan.

آ آ آ آ He yelped: ï؟½Have such things come to pass?
آ آ آ آ Howld hard there! Jerk ï؟½em up!
آ آ آ آ Put down yer packs from every ass,
آ آ آ آ And fork out Phaireyï؟½s cup!
آ آ آ آ It makes me sick, upon my soul,
آ آ آ آ The gratichood of man!
آ آ آ آ Ye had the feast, and then ye shtole
آ آ آ آ His silver billy-can.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ They swore that they had seen no cup,
آ آ آ آ And after each had sworn
آ آ آ آ They said the sandstorm coming up
آ آ آ آ Would simply spoil the corn.
آ آ آ آ They begged that he would wait until
آ آ آ آ They reached the nearest barn.
آ آ آ آ He said, ï؟½O thatï؟½s a wind that shook
آ آ آ آ The barley sort of yarn!


آ آ آ آ ï؟½(Now Iï؟½m no sergeant, understand,
آ آ آ آ Ye neednï؟½t call me that,
آ آ آ آ Oi want no sugar wid me sand
آ آ آ آ Whin Joseph smells a rat.)
آ آ آ آ Take down yer sacks from off yer backs,
آ آ آ آ The other asses too,
آ آ آ آ And rip the neck of every sack,
آ آ آ آ The boys will see yer through.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The cup was found in Benjaminï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ As all the worldï؟½s aware,
آ آ آ آ The constable seemed most surprised,
آ آ آ آ Because heï؟½d put it there.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½A greenhorn raised on assesï؟½ milk!
آ آ آ آ Well, this beats all I know!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And then, when he had cautioned them,
آ آ آ آ He took the gang in tow.

آ آ آ آ And when they started out to rend
آ آ آ آ Their turbans and their skirts,
آ آ آ آ He said, ï؟½Ye drunken lunatics,
آ آ آ آ Ye neednï؟½t tear yer shirts,
آ آ آ آ Yeï؟½re goinï؟½ where thereï؟½s ladies now,
آ آ آ آ So keep yer shirts on, mind.
آ آ آ آ (The Guvnor got in trouble wanst
آ آ آ آ For leavinï؟½ his behind.)ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And Joseph gaoled and frightened them.
آ آ آ آ (The ï؟½feastï؟½ was not amiss:
آ آ آ آ It showed him most magnanimous
آ آ آ آ With all that wasnï؟½t his.)
آ آ آ آ He took some extra graveyard pulls
آ آ آ آ At his old Dadï؟½s grey hairs,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Til Judah spoke up like a man,
آ آ آ آ And spoke up unawares.

آ آ آ آ Then Joseph said that he was Joe,
آ آ آ آ With Egypt in his clutch,
آ آ آ آ You will not be surprised to know
آ آ آ آ It didnï؟½t cheer them much.
آ آ آ آ And when he saw they were afraid,
آ آ آ آ And bowed beneath the rod,
آ آ آ آ He summoned snuffle to his aid,
آ آ آ آ And put it all on God.

آ آ آ آ And now the brethren understood,
آ آ آ آ With keen regret, no doubt,
آ آ آ آ That sin is seldom any good
آ آ آ آ Unless itï؟½s carried out.
آ آ آ آ For after that heart-breaking trip
آ آ آ آ Across the scorching sands
آ آ آ آ They found themselves in Josephï؟½s grip,
آ آ آ آ With Benny on their hands.

آ آ آ آ (Poor Reuben, to persuade his dad
آ آ آ آ To let the youngster come,
آ آ آ آ Had left his own sonsï؟½ lives in pledge
آ آ آ آ For Benjamin, at home.
آ آ آ آ But life is made of many fires
آ آ آ آ And countless frying-pans,
آ آ آ آ As fast as we get rid of Joeï؟½s
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re plagued by Benjaminï؟½s.)

آ آ آ آ Joe had a use for them, so he
آ آ آ آ Bade them to have no fear.
آ آ آ آ He said to them, ï؟½It was not you,
آ آ آ آ But God, who sent me here.
آ آ آ آ He sent me on to save your lives;
آ آ آ آ He hath sent you to me,
آ آ آ آ To see to you and all your wives,
آ آ آ آ And your posterity.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The Lord God hath exalted me,
آ آ آ آ And made me His right hand,
آ آ آ آ A father unto Pharaoh, and
آ آ آ آ A ruler in the land,
آ آ آ آ And likewise lord of Egyptï؟½,
آ آ آ آ He said a few things more,
آ آ آ آ And then he got to business straight,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve heard such cant before.

آ آ آ آ Those who have read will understand
آ آ آ آ I never mean to scoff,
آ آ آ آ But I hate all hypocrisy
آ آ آ آ And blasted showing-off.
آ آ آ آ How cunningly our holy Joe
آ آ آ آ Fixed up his tribeï؟½s affairs
آ آ آ آ For his own ends, and sprang the job
آ آ آ آ On Pharaoh unawares.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½The fame was heard in Pharaohï؟½s house,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Where peace and kindness thrived,
آ آ آ آ Saying, ï؟½Josephï؟½s brethren are comeï؟½
آ آ آ آ (Joeï؟½s brothers have arrived).
آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh heard, and was well pleased,
آ آ آ آ For he was white all through.
آ آ آ آ (And Moses says, without remark,
آ آ آ آ It pleased the servants too.)

آ آ آ آ But Pharaoh promptly put an end
آ آ آ آ To Josephï؟½s mummery.
آ آ آ آ He said, ï؟½Send waggons up, and bid
آ آ آ آ Thy people come to me.
آ آ آ آ Thou art commanded! Furnish them
آ آ آ آ With money and with food;
آ آ آ آ And say that I will give them land,
آ آ آ آ And see that it is good.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And Jacobï؟½s sons chucked up their runs
آ آ آ آ With blessings short and grim,
آ آ آ آ And Jacob took the stock and gear
آ آ آ آ And all his seed with him.
آ آ آ آ They sent the family tree ahead,
آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh read that same
آ آ آ آ (They found him very tired, ï؟½twas said,
آ آ آ آ And misty when they came).

آ آ آ آ And Pharaoh unto Joseph spake
آ آ آ آ Most kind, though wearily:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Thy father and thy brethren all
آ آ آ آ Are now come unto thee;
آ آ آ آ And Egypt is before thee now,
آ آ آ آ So in the best land make
آ آ آ آ Thy father and thy brethren dwell,
آ آ آ آ The land of Goshen take;

آ آ آ آ ï؟½And there, unhindered, let them thrive,
آ آ آ آ In comfort let them dwell,
آ آ آ آ Apart and free. My people love
آ آ آ آ All shepherds none too well,
آ آ آ آ But if thou knowest amongst them men
آ آ آ آ Of proved activity,
آ آ آ آ Then make them rulers over all
آ آ آ آ My flocks and herds for me.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ They brought five brethren unto him,
آ آ آ آ And he was very kind,
آ آ آ آ Perhaps he looked those brethren through,
آ آ آ آ And saw what lay behind.
آ آ آ آ His head he rested on his hand,
آ آ آ آ And smoothed his careworn brow,
آ آ آ آ He gazed on Israel thoughtfully,
آ آ آ آ And asked, ï؟½How old art thou?ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And Jacob told him, and was touched.
آ آ آ آ He said his days were few
آ آ آ آ And evil. They had not attained
آ آ آ آ To those his father knew.
آ آ آ آ But Jacob only had himself,
آ آ آ آ And no one else, to thank
آ آ آ آ If Joe had given his grey hairs
آ آ آ آ A second graveyard yank.

آ آ آ آ I think that Pharaoh was a man
آ آ آ آ Who always understood,
آ آ آ آ But was content to stand aside
آ آ آ آ If for his peopleï؟½s good,
آ آ آ آ And seem not missed the while. He knew
آ آ آ آ His merits, and no pride,
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½twas a grievous day for Jew
آ آ آ آ And Gentile when he died.



آ آ آ آ You know the rest of Josephï؟½s tale,
آ آ آ آ And well the poor Egyptians knew,
آ آ آ آ House agent on the grand old scale,
آ آ آ آ He boodled till the land was blue.
آ آ آ آ He squeezed them tight, and bled them white,
آ آ آ آ .آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ  .آ آ آ آ  .
آ آ آ آ Until a Pharaoh came in sight
آ آ آ آ Who didnï؟½t know him from a crow.

آ آ آ آ The Patriarchs, right back from Dad
آ آ آ آ To where the line begins,
آ آ آ آ Were great at passing ï؟½blessingsï؟½ on,
آ آ آ آ Together with their sins.
آ آ آ آ Old Noah was about the first,
آ آ آ آ Cursed Ham till all was blue,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½twas with some effect he cursed,
آ آ آ آ And with good reason too.

آ آ آ آ And when the time had come to pass
آ آ آ آ For Jacob to be gone,
آ آ آ آ He polished up his fatherï؟½s sins
آ آ آ آ And calmly passed them on.
آ آ آ آ He called his twelve sons round his bed
آ آ آ آ (Lest some good might befall),
آ آ آ آ He called his twelve sons to be blessed,
آ آ آ آ And cursed them, one and all

آ آ آ آ Save Joseph; and the rest had cause
آ آ آ آ To curse him ere they got
آ آ آ آ The English, who have every day
آ آ آ آ More cause to damn the lot.
آ آ آ آ And if they crossed the Red Sea now,
آ آ آ آ I guess weï؟½d let them go,
آ آ آ آ With ï؟½Satan hurry Kohensteinï؟½
آ آ آ آ And ï؟½God speed Ikey Mo!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ And lest my Jewish friends be wroth,
آ آ آ آ As they wonï؟½t be with me,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ll say that there is Jewish blood
آ آ آ آ In my posterity.
آ آ آ آ This verse, I trust, shall profit him
آ آ آ آ When he has ceased to grow,
آ آ آ آ My firstborn, who was known as ï؟½Jimï؟½,
آ آ آ آ But whose true name is ï؟½Joeï؟½.



AFTERWORD

آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve written much that is to blame,
آ آ آ آ But I have only sought to show
آ آ آ آ That hearts of men were just the same
آ آ آ آ Some forty centuries ago.
آ آ آ آ All kindness comes with womanï؟½s love,
آ آ آ آ That which she claims is due to her,
آ آ آ آ Not man! not man! but God above
آ آ آ آ Dare judge the wife of Potiphar.

آ آ آ آ And Jacob shall be ever blind
آ آ آ آ To reason and posterity,
آ آ آ آ In that ï؟½fond follyï؟½ of mankind
آ آ آ آ That is born of impotency.
آ آ آ آ No parentsï؟½ love or parentsï؟½ wealth
آ آ آ آ Shall ever fairly portioned be,
آ آ آ آ Faith shall not come, except by stealth,
آ آ آ آ Nor justice in one family.

آ آ آ آ And Joseph proved unto this hour,
آ آ آ آ Just what he was in Holy Writ,
آ آ آ آ A selfish tyrant in his power,
آ آ آ آ And, up or down, a hypocrite.
آ آ آ آ And Joseph still, whateï؟½er befall,
آ آ آ آ But gives his place to Benjamin,
آ آ آ آ And Reuben bears the brunt of all,
آ آ آ آ Though Judah does the best he can.

آ آ آ آ The hearts of men shall never change
آ آ آ آ While one man dies and one is born,
آ آ آ آ We journey yet, though ways seem strange,
آ آ آ آ Down into Egypt to buy corn.
آ آ آ آ Some prosper there, and they forget;
آ آ آ آ And some go down, and are forgot;
آ آ آ آ And Pride and Self betray us yet,
آ آ آ آ Till Pharaohs rise that know us not.

آ آ آ آ But kindliness shall live for aye,
آ آ آ آ And, though we well our fate deserve,
آ آ آ آ Samaritans shall pass that way,
آ آ آ آ And kings like Pharaoh rule to serve.
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½re fighting out of Egyptï؟½s track,
آ آ آ آ And, ah! the fight is ever grand,
آ آ آ آ Although, in Canaan or Out Back,
آ آ آ آ We never reach the Promised Land.
meaning:
"Josephï؟½s Dreams And Reubenï؟½s Brethren (A Recital In Six Chapters)" is a quintessential example of Henry Lawson's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
